## List of Permitted Supplemental Ingredients
- Supersedes: 2025-01-30
- Date issued: 2025-11-25
Access archived versions of this list.
The List of Permitted Supplemental Ingredients is incorporated by reference in the Food and Drug Regulations .
This document lists all the supplemental ingredients permitted for addition to supplemented foods, along with their conditions of use. These conditions include:
- the food category to which the supplemental ingredient may be added;
- the maximum amount and the units per serving of stated size for a supplemental ingredient when added to a food belonging to the specified food category;
- the cautionary statements required on the label for each supplemental ingredient and the threshold level above which these statements are required; and
- any other conditions of use or additional labelling requirements related to the supplemental ingredient
Certain ingredients are not permitted for addition to supplemented foods at any level and additional restrictions with respect to the addition of certain supplemental ingredients may apply.
Additional information on supplemental ingredients, their conditions of use, and the process to amend the List to add a new supplemental ingredient is provided in the Guidance document for Supplemented Foods Regulations .

## Who this document is for
- Other interested stakeholders

(1) (a) When beta-carotene and retinol and/or its derivatives are present in the supplemented food, the following requirements apply: For products containing vitamin A (beta-carotene) as a supplemental ingredient, where non- supplemental sources of retinol and/or retinyl esters are present, retinol and/or retinyl esters must be declared as vitamin A (retinol) under the "Supplemented with" heading in the Supplemented Food Facts table; When both vitamin A (beta-carotene) and vitamin A (retinol) are declared under the "Supplemented with" heading, the amount in micrograms for each form of Vitamin A must be declared separately. However, a single percent Daily Value (DV) declaration is required that accounts for both forms of vitamin A. The single percent DV is determined based on the sum of both forms of vitamin A in micrograms of retinol activity equivalents (RAE), calculated on the basis of the relationships set out in paragraph D.01.003 (1)(a). The percent DV must be centred against the amounts of vitamin A (beta-carotene) and vitamin A (retinol) in micrograms that appears to the left of it, as follows: The amount in micrograms of each form of vitamin A is rounded off to the nearest whole number. The single percent DV that accounts for both forms of vitamin A, is rounded off as follows: if the sum of both forms of vitamin A, calculated on the basis of micrograms of RAE, is "0", to 0%; and in all other cases, to the nearest multiple of 1% (b) When beta-carotene is the only form of vitamin A present in the supplemented food, the percent DV is determined based on the amount of vitamin A (veta-carotene) in micrograms of RAE, calculated on the basis of the relationship set out in paragraph D.01.003 (1)(a)
